# Conclusions and Future Work {#sec-conclusions}

This chapter steps back from the technical details of the previous four chapters and asks three things: what MaintAlign actually accomplished, where its results fall short of what a mature research system should deliver, and what comes next for anyone who wants to extend the work. The discussion stays close to the evidence already presented in @sec-results rather than introducing new claims. Where the evidence is strong, the chapter says so plainly. Where the evidence is thin, the chapter says that too. The last section revisits the ethical concerns raised in @sec-introduction now that the experiments have actually been run and there are concrete numbers to discuss.

## Summary of Contributions and Results {#sec-summary-results}

The research problem behind MaintAlign can be stated in one sentence. Given a set of machines with known failure distributions, a fixed pool of technicians, a planning horizon, and a set of production chains that link machines together, find the preventive maintenance schedule that minimizes total expected cost. Cost here means more than the dollar amount of the maintenance itself. It also includes the expected cost of breakdowns from skipped or delayed maintenance, the production loss during maintenance windows, and the retooling cost when a chain restarts after one of its machines is serviced. No single off-the-shelf tool handles all four of those together under shared-resource constraints, which is the gap the project tries to fill.

Three contributions came out of the work, each described in detail in earlier chapters.

The first is the **constraint-based optimization model** in @sec-methodology. The CP-SAT formulation makes each potential maintenance task an optional interval variable, so the solver decides both how many times each machine is serviced and when each service event happens. Failure risk is modeled per machine using a Weibull distribution, and imperfect repair is handled through Kijima Type I virtual age. Most prior maintenance scheduling work assumes perfect repair, so the Kijima step is one of the more meaningful modeling choices in the project. Production chain costs and retooling costs are encoded with half-reified Boolean variables that give the solver an incentive to cluster same-chain maintenance events into opportunistic groups. Expected Weibull costs are precomputed into scaled integer tables, which keeps floating-point work out of the search loop.

The second contribution is the **empirical evidence** in @sec-results. Three experiments were run across four problem sizes, each repeated with three random seeds. The CP-SAT optimizer produced the cheapest schedule at every size tested, beating four baselines (max-interval, half-max, analytical Weibull-optimal, and condition-based) by 17.9% on the small instance and by 23.5--25.4% on the medium and large instances. Monte Carlo simulation with 500 random failure draws per schedule confirmed that those savings were not artifacts of using deterministic cost estimates: the optimized schedules stayed cheapest under random failures and showed lower VaR95 tail risk than every baseline at every size. On solve time, CP-SAT returned OPTIMAL status in under three minutes for ten-machine instances on two of three seeds, and a feasible-but-not-provably-optimal solution on the third.

The third contribution is **a usable artifact, not just a solver script**. Most research on maintenance optimization ends with a solver log and a table of costs. MaintAlign ships with a Streamlit dashboard that loads machine data from CSV, runs the optimizer, and renders interactive Gantt charts, cost breakdowns, and Monte Carlo risk distributions. A plant engineer with no background in constraint programming can upload a spreadsheet and get a schedule in under a minute. Whether or not anyone in industry ever uses this dashboard, its existence made the research easier to explain to non-specialist audiences and supported a three-minute presentation at the Conference on Emerging Research.

Taken together, these three pieces answer the research question from @sec-introduction. Constraint programming, combined with Weibull-based cost preprocessing and opportunistic chain grouping, is a practical approach for resource-constrained preventive maintenance scheduling at small-to-medium scales. The measured savings are large enough to matter in practice, and the solver is fast enough to be useful as an interactive planning tool rather than an overnight batch job.

## Significance and Limitations {#sec-limitations}

The experimental story is favorable, but several honest qualifications belong here before anyone treats MaintAlign as a finished answer. Being clear about what the experiments cannot show is not false modesty; it is how future researchers know which claims they can build on and which ones still need more evidence.

The most important caveat is that every instance was **synthetic**. All factory configurations came from the `utils/generator.py` module, with Weibull parameters, cost ratios, and chain structures drawn from plausible but artificial ranges. No real factory data was used at any point. The generator was designed to produce feasible, realistic-looking instances across difficulty levels, but realistic-looking is not the same as matching any specific plant. A facility whose machines deviate systematically from a Weibull wear-out pattern --- for example, one where failures are dominated by random external events rather than gradual wear --- could see the savings shrink, or in some cases grow, by amounts these experiments cannot predict. Until MaintAlign is validated against at least one real industrial dataset, the 18--25% savings figure should be read as evidence that the approach has promise, not as a forecast for any specific facility.

A second caveat is the **scale ceiling around ten machines**. The scalability experiment in @sec-results tested up to twenty machines and reported OPTIMAL status only up to ten machines within the 180-second time budget. Beyond that, the solver returned feasible solutions with bounded optimality gaps, and the gap widens as the problem grows. Real industrial maintenance problems often involve fifty, a hundred, or several hundred machines. The decomposition module described in @sec-methodology is a first response --- it splits the factory into chain-based subproblems and merges the results --- but the merging step uses a simple technician-conflict heuristic, not a proof of global optimality. Any claim about scale above twenty machines should therefore be treated as preliminary.

The four baselines in @sec-results also deserve a closer look. They are reasonable rules of thumb from the literature, but they are not the strongest possible competitors. A well-tuned mixed-integer program, a genetic algorithm with opportunistic-grouping operators, or a reinforcement learning policy could all potentially match or beat the CP-SAT optimizer. The reported savings are relative to the specific baselines implemented here, and those baselines were built by the same person who built the optimizer. They represent reasonable effort, but not an adversarial attempt to make the baselines as strong as possible. The Gurobi head-to-head proposed in @sec-future-work would be a much stronger test.

Two smaller limitations are worth recording before moving on. The Streamlit dashboard does not yet wrap CSV upload, solver invocation, or Monte Carlo runs in user-friendly error handlers, so an invalid CSV produces a Python traceback rather than a clear error banner, and solver timeouts are surfaced less cleanly than they should be. These are software quality issues rather than research issues, but they affect any claim about practitioner usability; the fix is identified in @sec-future-engineering. The literature review in @sec-related-work was also constrained to ACM-indexed sources by an institutional requirement. ACM's coverage of maintenance optimization is sparse compared to industrial engineering and reliability journals like *Reliability Engineering & System Safety* or the *European Journal of Operational Research*, where much of the foundational work on age-replacement, imperfect repair, and multi-component maintenance has been published. As a result, the related-work chapter leans more on constraint-programming-for-scheduling and simulation-for-validation than on a broad maintenance-optimization survey, and claims of novelty should be read against that narrower comparison set.

## Future Work {#sec-future-work}

The limitations above map directly onto future research directions. The list below is ordered roughly by how much new effort each item would require, from engineering improvements that a summer could finish to longer extensions that would fit a senior thesis or a first graduate project.

### Engineering and Usability Extensions {#sec-future-engineering}

The most immediate task is to **complete the error handling on the dashboard**. Every public entry point --- CSV upload, solver invocation, Monte Carlo run --- should validate its input through a shared `core/validators.py` module and surface problems through `st.error` banners with concrete suggestions for how to fix the input. Solver timeouts should produce `st.warning` messages that suggest the next step (increase the time limit, reduce the problem size, or switch to decomposition mode). The work is not technically deep, but it is a hard prerequisite for any future claim that MaintAlign is usable outside a research setting.

A second engineering direction is **integrating real sensor data**. Most modern industrial plants instrument their equipment with sensors that stream vibration, temperature, and current draw into historian databases. A sensor integration layer would replace MaintAlign's static Weibull parameters with online estimates updated from those streams. A reasonable evaluation would compare two versions of the same schedule on the same real dataset: one using Weibull parameters estimated from the first half of the data, and one using parameters updated daily from live readings over the second half. If the sensor-informed schedule reduces realized cost by a measurable amount, the integration earns its place.

A third extension is **spare parts constraints**. MaintAlign currently assumes that whenever a maintenance task is scheduled, the parts needed to complete it are on hand. In real plants, parts must be ordered, inventoried, and sometimes shared across machines. Adding a per-period inventory variable and a consumption constraint per task would let the solver trade off the cost of holding parts against the risk of schedule delays when parts are missing. This extension would roughly double the constraint count, so a solve-time study would need to accompany the feature.

### Research Questions That Extend the Empirical Contribution {#sec-future-research}

The single most valuable research extension is a **Gurobi head-to-head** on the same instance suite from @sec-results. Gurobi is the leading commercial mixed-integer solver and has known strengths on problems with strong linear-programming relaxations; CP-SAT, by contrast, is built for combinatorial scheduling with tight bounds. Running both solvers with the same time limit, the same warm-start information, and the same random seeds would give direct evidence about which solver family is better suited to resource-constrained preventive maintenance. A fair evaluation would report three metrics: time to first feasible solution, time to proven optimality, and objective value at the time limit when optimality is not reached. A result in either direction is publishable: if CP-SAT wins, that supports the choice of constraint programming for this problem class; if Gurobi wins, it gives a clear modeling target for future CP-SAT improvements.

A complementary research extension is a **scaling study at 25, 50, and 100 machines**. The existing `generate_industrial` and `generate_factory` presets already produce instances in those size ranges, but none of them appear in @sec-results because the solver could not finish them inside the time budget. A proper scaling study would run the monolithic solver, the chain-based decomposer, and possibly a Gurobi formulation on each size, with enough seeds (at least ten) to estimate variance. The central question is the size at which the monolithic solver stops producing useful schedules, and whether the decomposer degrades gracefully or fails abruptly beyond that point. The reporting targets would be solve time, optimality gap at timeout, and savings over the best baseline at each size.

A third direction is **multi-objective optimization**. The current objective collapses four cost components into a single weighted sum with weights determined by the input data. A decision maker who treats cost and risk separately --- for example, a chemical plant where a single breakdown may have safety consequences out of proportion to its dollar cost --- needs a Pareto frontier, not a single point estimate. Extending MaintAlign to produce such a frontier, either by varying the weights or by using an explicit multi-objective formulation, would support a more informed conversation with domain experts. A natural evaluation would compare the frontier from the multi-objective version against a grid of single-objective solves at different weights, and report the dominance relationship between them.

The most ambitious direction is **dynamic rescheduling**. The schedule today is computed once at the start of the horizon and then followed. In reality, machines fail unexpectedly, parts arrive late, and technicians call in sick. A dynamic rescheduler would re-run the optimization at any disruption, using the remaining horizon and current machine states as the new input, and splice the re-solved tail onto the portion already executed. A rolling-horizon simulation would evaluate this: repeatedly draw random breakdowns from the Weibull distributions, re-solve at each breakdown, and compare the realized cost to both the static schedule and a myopic "fix whatever just broke next" heuristic. If the rescheduled policy achieves lower realized cost with acceptable compute overhead, dynamic rescheduling moves from thought experiment to a real feature.

## Ethical Implications Revisited {#sec-ethics}

@sec-introduction discussed ethical considerations at the design stage, when they were still mostly hypothetical. Now that the experiments in @sec-results have produced concrete results, three of those considerations deserve a second look.

The first is that **technician welfare is not in the model**. MaintAlign treats the technician pool as a shared integer resource with capacity $K$. That abstraction is convenient for the solver, but it hides a real ethical question: a schedule that squeezes the last percentage point of cost out of the maintenance budget may do so by packing technicians tightly, eliminating slack, or pushing work into nights and weekends. The current tool has no representation of any of this. A responsible industrial deployment should extend the model with at least three soft constraints: a penalty for off-hours work that discourages but does not forbid night and weekend tasks, a minimum-rest constraint between consecutive tasks for the same technician, and a per-technician workload cap across the horizon. Each constraint would have a tunable weight so that a facility could calibrate the trade-off between cost and labor conditions rather than optimizing only for dollars.

The second is that **input quality is a real failure mode**. MaintAlign's output is only as good as its Weibull parameters, cost estimates, and chain definitions. A facility that supplies optimistic cost ratios or understates breakdown costs will get schedules that skip valuable maintenance, and the optimizer will confidently report that those schedules are cheapest. If the tool is released widely, some users will trust the output without scrutinizing the inputs. Two mitigations should ship by default: a sensitivity analysis script that shows how much the optimal schedule changes when Weibull parameters shift by plausible amounts, and a clearer dashboard signal when the solver returns FEASIBLE rather than OPTIMAL --- because in that case the reported cost is only an upper bound, not a proven optimum.

The third is that **safety-critical machines should not be cost-optimized**. The maintenance intervals MaintAlign recommends are driven purely by expected cost, not by the severity of a potential failure. For most machines this is fine. For a pressure vessel whose failure could injure workers, it is not: such equipment should be maintained on a worst-case-risk basis, not an expected-cost basis. Any industrial adopter of MaintAlign should classify each machine as either cost-optimized or safety-optimized, and the tool should enforce a tighter maximum-interval constraint for the safety-optimized group. The model already supports per-machine maximum intervals through the `max_interval` parameter, but the feature needs explicit documentation and policy language rather than being buried as a generic knob.

A small but important final point is that the open-source release should include a license that clarifies permitted uses, a statement that the tool is a research prototype not validated for safety-critical deployment, and pointers to the sensitivity analysis and Monte Carlo modules so that users understand the uncertainty in their results. These additions are small individually, but together they nudge the default usage pattern away from "run it and trust the output" and toward something more cautious.

## Closing {#sec-closing}

The central claim of this research is that constraint programming is an effective tool for a class of maintenance optimization problems that prior approaches handle either incompletely, by ignoring chain dependencies and technician limits, or impractically, by demanding expensive sensor infrastructure. The results in @sec-results support that claim at small-to-medium scales. They do not support stronger claims about industrial-scale performance, real-data fidelity, or superiority to commercial solvers, and this chapter has tried to be clear about that boundary.

What the project does well is hold together three pieces that usually live in separate papers: a principled reliability model, a constraint-based solver with opportunistic grouping, and an interface that a non-specialist can actually operate. Building MaintAlign over two semesters was as much an exercise in research discipline --- in framing results honestly, in noticing the gap between plausible and proven, in keeping a working prototype distinct from a deployable system --- as it was a constraint programming project. The natural next step, taken during senior-level work in the Computer and Information Science department, is to close that gap on at least one of the dimensions identified above: real data, larger scale, or a stronger solver comparison. Whichever comes first, the foundation built in this report is the starting point.
